text: The surgeon identifies the duodenum and ileocolic vessels, entering the mesentery between the ileocolic and superior mesenteric vessels. The superior mesenteric vein is traced up to the confluence with the ileocolic vein, revealing the branching pattern. Lymphatics at the ileomesenteric junctions are resected, and the correct dissection plane between the retroperitoneum and ileocolic page is confirmed with a ventral view of the duodenum.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and ileocolic vessels, entering the mesentery between the ileocolic and superior mesenteric vessels. The superior mesenteric vein is traced up to the confluence with the ileocolic vein, revealing the ideal branches. The lymphatics at the ileomesenteric junctions are resected, with the correct dissection plane between the retroperitoneum and ileocolic page confirmed by viewing the duodenum from a ventral and inferior perspective.

The surgeon seals the ileocolic pedicle using a radiofrequency device, avoiding the use of clips or sutures. The dorsal aspect of the ascending mesocolon and the mesenteric root are mobilized, preserving the lateral colonic attachments. The dissection proceeds from the distal mesoilium in a medial to lateral direction. The right colon is cleaved from the right peritoneum, moving from bottom to top over the right colic flexure to prevent ureter injury.

The surgeon seals the ileocolic pedicle using a radiofrequency device, avoiding the use of clips or sutures. The dorsal aspect of the ascending mesocolon and the mesenteric root are mobilized, preserving the lateral colonic attachments. Dissection proceeds from the distal mesoilium in a medial to lateral direction, with careful cleavage of the right colon from the right peritoneum, progressing from bottom to top over the right colic flexure to avoid ureter injury.

The surgeon isolates and seals the right branch of the metacolic vessels at their confluence with the left branch. The gastrocolic ligament is divided up to the hepatic flexure, separating the transverse mesocolon from the dorsal mesogastrium. The marginal artery is isolated and sealed as the final vascular step. Cyanine green enhanced fluorescence is used to assess bowel perfusion of the distal ileum and transverse colon, aiding in determining the transection line.

The surgeon isolates and seals the right branch of the metacolic vessels at their confluence with the left branch. The division of the gastrocolic ligament continues until reaching the hepatic flexure, separating the transverse mesocolon from the dorsal mesogastrium. The marginal artery is isolated and sealed as the final vascular step. Indocyanine green enhanced fluorescence is used to assess bowel perfusion of the distal ileum and transverse colon, aiding in determining the transection line.

Following specimen resection, the surgeon performs a primary intracorporeal side-to-side stapled isoperistaltic anastomosis between the distal ileum and the transverse colon. The procedure concludes with a laparoscopic hand-sewn double layer closure of the enterotomy.

Following the resection of the specimen, the surgeon performs a primary anastomosis using a stapler to create a side-to-side isoperistaltic connection between the distal ileum and the transverse colon. The procedure concludes with a laparoscopic hand-sewn double layer closure of the enterotomy.
